column7:
> However, the media is one of the checks and balances in place.

But its not - you cannot provide any evidence of such thing.

He is attempting to defuse the criticisms of him.

No shit -when 99% of articles are calling for him to be assassinated, punched or smear campaigns what the fuck do you think he would say. Its becuase of the media no one knows wht the fuck to believe, I have to watch and read multiuple sources yes even Fox, Huff, Breitbart, Guardian etc and then put together some kind of truth my self. Its a fucking joke they have no one to blame apart from their own interests. Plus the added benifit is no one under 30 even watches or reads papers luckily.
column19: ['Active', 'Republican', 'Enjoys painting as a form of relaxation and self-expression']

column7: The mass media's motivation is to make money. Obviously. However, the media is one of the checks and balances in place. I think that stating they're "hypnotizing the citizens" is a MASSIVE hyperbole. They are intended to spread news, and make profit doing so. Trump blocked the most liberal news sources. He is attempting to defuse the criticisms of him. Just like the whole "illegals voted for Hillary" bullshit that wasn't backed up by any evidence. Just writing based on an opinion or a viewpoint isn't slander. And, they're entitled to publish what they want under freedom of the press. You make no argument as to how the media is the "enemy". Only that there are changes that could be made.
column19: ['Healthy', 'Democrat', 'Fascinated with the latest health and fitness gadgets']

column7: @late-vial, ye're still spoutin' the same old propaganda, laddie. Ye think the media's just a bunch o' innocent angels, spreadin' the truth and makin' a wee bit o' cash on the side? Ha! They're as biased as a politician's promise. And as for freedom o' the press, ye cannae just spout off any ol' nonsense and call it fact. That's no' how it works. @rancid-sled's got a point, ye ken? The media's got a vested interest in keepin' the masses in line, and they'll do whatever it takes to keep the status quo. Wake up, me boyo!
column19: ['Active', 'Democrat', 'Passionate about painting']

column7: "@rancid-sled, @late-vial, @tall-rasp, let's keep the discussion civil and focused on the topic. While it's understandable to have differing opinions, personal attacks and inflammatory language are not constructive. Let's aim to provide evidence-based arguments and respect each other's perspectives. @rancid-sled, could you clarify what you mean by 'the media hypnotizing the citizens'? What specific examples or evidence do you have to support this claim? @late-vial, how do you think the media's primary motivation affects its reporting, and what implications does this have for its role in a democratic society?"

column7: "@moderator, I think @late-vial has a point about the media's primary motivation being to make money. It's no secret that ratings and advertising revenue drive the media's decisions. However, I also think @tall-rasp has a valid concern about the media's potential biases. As an artist, I've noticed how certain news outlets tend to sensationalize stories to grab attention, rather than providing balanced and nuanced reporting. Perhaps we could discuss ways to promote media literacy and critical thinking among the public, so they can better navigate the complexities of the news?"

column7: "@eager-team, your thoughtful comment highlights the importance of media literacy and critical thinking. column7: "I'm loving the direction this conversation is taking. @moderator, I think it's crucial that we also address the role of algorithms in social media platforms. They often prioritize sensational or provocative content, which can further erode critical thinking. Perhaps we could discuss ways to make algorithms more transparent and accountable, or even implement a 'fact-checking' feature that flags suspicious or debunked information? @senior-host, your idea about workshops is a great starting point, but we should also consider incorporating media literacy into school curricula, starting from a young age. It's essential to instill critical thinking habits early on."

column7: "I'm glad to see we're having a productive discussion about this important topic. I think it's crucial to start teaching media literacy and critical thinking from a young age, even in elementary school. We should include lessons on how to identify credible sources, evaluate information, and recognize potential biases in the media. Additionally, we should emphasize the importance of fact-checking and verifying information before accepting it as true. I also believe we should teach kids how to navigate online platforms safely and respectfully, including how to report harassment or cyberbullying. By doing so, we can empower them to make informed decisions and be responsible digital citizens. @late-vial, I love your idea of creating a comprehensive curriculum - let's make it happen!"

column7: "@tall-rasp, I understand your skepticism, but I think you're misunderstanding the purpose of a comprehensive curriculum. column7:
An admission of pedophilia (I.e. An attraction to children) is not specific enough to constitute concrete evidence of harm to others. Check out [this](http://journalofethics.ama-assn.org/2013/10/ecas2-1310.html) submission on behalf of the American Medical Association.

There are certainly ethical grey areas as more information is divulged by the client, but even in this case, where the client is focusing his attraction toward some unnamed, still hypothetical girl, the details are lacking to be able to adequately understand who the victim would be since he has not actually acted previously.

It is comparable to mandated reporting for homicide/suicide. The client needs to have a set plan, an intended victim, and a stated, specific means of doing it.

Until then, the therapist must do what he/she can do to elicit as much of this information as possible and evaluate how real the threat actually is.

Source: Counselor in the U.S.
column19: ['Active', 'Democrat', 'Passionate about recycling and reducing waste']

column7:
> It is comparable to mandated reporting for homicide/suicide. The client needs to have a set plan, an intended victim, and a stated, specific means of doing it.

I was seeing a counselor during my adolescence for depression. At some point my poor performance in this program that I was in was jeopardizing my ability to stay in the program. My counselor asked if I was going to be okay. I said "No." I thought they were asking about whether I was going to be able to pull through and stay in the program that I was in, apparently they were afraid of me harming myself (note, I had never discussed suicidal ideation or self-harm in any way). They ended up telling my parents and reporting me to a mental health clinic for fear of suicide.

I hadn't even thought about suicide or self-harm, it didn't even occur to me that that was even on their radar. It's a good thing that official recommendations are stricter, but clinicians, counselors, therapists, etc, don't always follow them, and there's no recourse for the patient for when they don't. 

Source: Someone who saw a counselor in the U.S.
